title: Informed Trading and Intertemporal Substitution
abstract: I examine the possibility of information-based trading in a multiperiod consumption setting. I develop a necessary and sufficient condition for trade to occur. Intertemporal substitution introduces a desire to correlate current consumption with future aggregate shocks. When agents have heterogeneous time-inseparable preferences, information differentially affects relative preferences for current and future consumption, making information-based trading mutually acceptable. The no-trade result continues to hold if there is no aggregate shock, or if agents have either homogeneous or time-separable preferences.

article.introduction:
Each day we observe asignificant volume of trades in the markets. The no-trade theorem, however, has established that when the market is complete for all endowment shocks and agents are rational and share the same prior beliefs, it is impossible to generate trades for purely informational reasons, even in the presence of asymmetric information.1Because the no-trade theorem serves as a key benchmark in financial economics, understanding its scope is important. In this paper, I extend the original single-period consumption no-trade model to multiperiod consumption settings and identify intertemporal consumption smoothing as a mechanism that generates information-based trading.
Dynamic consumption extensions are possibly the most natural way to check the robustness of results in a single-period consumption model. In the no-trade literature, however, dynamic consumption extensions are widely believed to be trivial. Because consumption in different periods can be treated as different goods in agents' consumption bundles, common sense suggests that when the market is complete across all endowment shocks, any multiperiod extension should be isomorphic to a corresponding single-period case with an appropriately defined consumption bundle. Thus, while researchers have considered many explanations for trade, such as heterogeneous beliefs, liquidity trading, and market incompleteness in endowment states, dynamic consumption generally has not been considered.
In this paper, I show that dynamic consumption extensions are nontrivial for two reasons. First, in addition to trading for mutual insurance across endowment shocks, agents trade to smooth their consumption over time. Concerns about intertemporal substitution incentivize agents to correlate their current consumption with future shocks. Second, a dynamic consumption extension imposes natural constraints on feasible allocations through ex-ante trading. The nature of time suggests that all securities are measurable to information filtration, and hence no security can pay different amounts of current consumption based on the realizations of future endowment shocks, even when the market is complete across all endowment shocks. As a result, agents are not able to correlate their current consumption with future shocks through ex-ante trading. Information-based trading, however, maybe mutually acceptable because it enables agents to adjust their consumption profiles based on additional information about future shocks. These adjustments allow agents to achieve some allocations that are infeasible ex-ante.
I study a simple exchange economy with multiperiod consumption and agents with Epstein-Zin-type preferences. I characterize a necessary and sufficient condition for trade to occur in dynamic consumption environments. Indeed, concerns about intertemporal substitution fail to generate trades under only three scenarios. In the first case, without future aggregate risk, agents hold the same position in all future endowment states, and hence, the realization of future states becomes irrelevant. In the second case, all agents have time-separable utility functions. With time-separable preferences, each agent's marginal utility on current consumption is independent of her future consumption, and her optimal allocation does not vary across future states. When agents have standard time and state-separable preferences (Milgrom and Stokey (1982), Tirole (1982)), the no-trade theorem holds in dynamic consumption environments. In the last case, agents have homogeneous preferences that lead them to smooth consumption in the same way, resulting in no gains from trade.
Given the Epstein-Zin-type preferences in the baseline model, one may wonder whether the trades are driven by nonexpected utility preferences. To address this concern, in SectionIII.B, I study a simple example with habit formation preferences. Habit formation preferences are dynamically consistent, are state-separable, and belong to the family of expected utility (EU) functions. I show that concerns about intertemporal substitution continue to generate trade because habit formation preferences are time-inseparable.
This paper contributes to the literature on information-based trading. Beginning with Aumann (1976), a series of no-trade theorems challenged the commonsense intuition of information-based trading (Kreps (1977), Grossman and Stiglitz (1980), Milgrom and Stokey (1982), Tirole (1982), Blume, Coury, and Easley (2006)). Researchers have thus considered many explanations for trade, including “irrational trading” by noisy traders (Grossman and Stiglitz (1980), Glosten and Milgrom (1985), Kyle (1985)), or heterogeneous beliefs across traders (Harrison and Kreps (1978), Morris (1994)). Grundy and McNichols (1989) argue that in a two-period noisy rational expectations equilibrium (REE) model, in one of the equilibrate, agents will trade and existing private information will be further impounded into prices in the second period. In Bond and Eraslan (2010), the value of a security depends on the buyer's action, while buyers take actions only after the trade. Many explanations proposed for rational trading are based on market incompleteness (Blume, Coury, and Easley (2006), Gottardi and Rahi (2013), Lubensky and Smith (2016)). Blume, Coury, and Easley (2006) examine trading when the market is incomplete with respect to endowment shocks. In Lubensky and Smith (2016), agents need to trade in one period to learn their own trading type and the benefit is so high that these new agents behave like noisy traders. In my paper, these trades come from the nature of time. Since securities are measurable to information filtration, a security cannot deliver different current payoffs contingent on future endowment shocks. Agents with a strong desire to smooth consumption over time may therefore agree to trade on a signal about future shocks and achieve different allocations based on the realization of that signal.
This paper is not the first to discuss preferences and the no-trade result. Dow, Madrigal, and Werlang (1990) show that if preferences are not state-additive, then agents may be dynamically inconsistent in which case it is possible to generate trade. Halevy (2004) demonstrates that trades are possible if agents have nonconsequential preferences. My paper identifies intertemporal substitution concerns as an additional source of trading.
This paper is also related to the literature on information value. Motivated by Hirshleifer (1971), several studies (Jaffe (1975), Ng (1975), Hakansson, Kunkel, and Ohlson (1982), Maurer and Tran (2016,2018)) examine the social value of an unexpected release of public information in a pure exchange economy. In the spirit of Milgrom and Stokey (1982), in this paper, I focus on the possibility of trading after the receipt of anticipated private information.
The rest of this paper is organized as follows: SectionIIintroduces the model setup. SectionIIanalyzes rational information-based trading. SectionIIIdiscusses information filtration and trading with EU. SectionIVconcludes.
article.I. The Model:
Consider a simple pure exchange economy with two agentsi∈{1,2}and three datest∈{0,1,2}. Each agent receives perishable endowments at dates 1 and 2, respectively. Let Ω be the set of finite possible states of the world, with a generic elementω≡(θ,x). The state of the world consists of two components: signal events,x∈{H,L}, and endowment events,θ∈{A,B}. The total endowment at date 1 is a constante1, whereas the date 2 total endowment depends on θ. Leteθ,θ∈{A,B}be the date 2 total endowment given the realized endowment state θ. Without loss of generality, I assumeeA≥eB. Leteji,j∈{1,A,B}be agenti's initial endowment position in statej. For eachj,ej1+ej2=ej. I focus on this simple setup to illustrate the fundamental mechanism. Extending the analysis to more general cases with finite many agents, states, and dates is straightforward.
Endowments are not directly affected by signal states. However,xand θ may be statistically related. Both agents share the common prior that endowment stateAhappens with probabilityπ0∈(0,1). At date 1, agent 1 observes a private signalxand forms her corresponding posteriorπx∈(0,1),x∈{H,L}. Agent 2 does not have private information. Let ω be the probability of observing signalH. Rational Bayesian updating implies thatωπH+(1−ω)πL=π0. Without loss of generality, I assumeπH>π0>πL.
Agents can trade in a financial market. The market opens at date 0, and agents can trade again at date 1 after agent 1 receives her private signalx. The market is competitive, and both agents are price takers. Three Arrow-Deberu securities trade in the financial market:S1is the claim on date 1 endowment, andSθ,θ∈{A,B}, are date 2 endowment claims conditional on corresponding endowment state θ. As in Milgrom and Stokey (1982), the financial market is complete on endowment events, but incomplete on signal events. In other words, the market allows for the full set of Arrow-Deberu securities contingent on all payoff-relevant state dates. If the financial market is complete on both endowment and signal states, then the model becomes a standard Arrow-Debreu model and it is then trivial to observe no trade at date 1.
Denote the price of securitySj,j∈{1,A,B}, at datetbypjt, and letpt=(p1t,pAt,pBt)be the timetprice vector of securities. Without loss of generality, I use date 1 goodsj=1as the numeraire. At date 1, only the signal statexis realized and pricespj1(x)are functions ofx. At date 1, agent 1 learns both date 1 pricesp1and her private signalx, whereas agent 2 only observes pricesp1. One can interpret agent 1 as an informed trader and agent 2 as an uninformed trader.
To illustrate the mechanism for information-based trade, in the analysis below, I focus on the case in which agents are price takers and the financial market is competitive.
Definition 1.An equilibrium consists of trading plansz*={zji*}and pricesp*={pjt*}such that:
article.II. Rational Information-Based Trading:
In this section, I discuss the possibility of information-based trade. For ease of notation, I drop the superscript “*” but all prices and trading plans refer to the equilibrium values unless noted otherwise.
I begin the analysis by demonstrating the existence and uniqueness of the equilibrium. The following lemma shows that it is also fully revealing. Without loss of generality, both agents share the same belief in the rest of the analysis.
Lemma 1.There exists an equilibrium with an interior allocation. Moreover, it is unique and fully revealing, that is,p1(H)≠p1(L).


Proof.See AppendixA.□

If there is no trading in equilibrium, it follows that the optimal allocation at date 1 is invariant to the realization of signal states. Similar to other no-trade models, whilezi,1=0, date 1's prices incorporate posterior updates. The price is fully revealing in a trading equilibrium because trades are mutually acceptable, suggesting a change in the Pareto-efficient allocation and associated marginal utility.
Following the literature, the analysis here focuses on the possibility of trading at date 1. The central result in the no-trade theorem is that when the market is complete with respect to endowment states, the arrival of date 1's private information cannot generate incentives to trade.
Definition 2.In equilibrium, the no-trade result holds ifzji,1=0,∀(θ,x)∈Ω,j∈{1,A,B},i=1,2.

Definition2formally characterizes the no-trade scenario. The following lemma gives an alternative characterization of the no-trade result.
Lemma 2.In equilibrium, the no-trade result holds if and only if



Proof.See AppendixA.□

Under the alternative interpretation of the no-trade result in Lemma2, the arrival of private information at date 1 does not change the optimal allocation. In other words, the Pareto-optimal allocation (conditional on signalx) does not vary across signal states. Because the invariant allocation is optimal for each possible realization of signalx, it is also ex-ante Pareto-optimal.
Given the preferences, endowments, and common prior π0, letΠbe the set of all possible pairs of posteriors(πH,πL),0<πL<π0<πH<1. The main result of this paper is to establish a necessary and sufficient condition for date 1 trade to occur in dynamic consumption environments.
Theorem 1.If all of the following conditions hold, then date 1 trade occurs for almost all probabilities(πH,πL)∈Π:



Proof.See AppendixA.□

The sufficient part of Theorem1shows that when all three conditions hold, rational information-based trade exists. The proof of Theorem1further shows that the no-trade result is negligible because, for eachπH, the no-trade result holds at most for one particularπL. Information-based trade is general in the sense that three conditions above all involve highly restrictive parameter constraints. Information-based trade is mutually acceptable because it allows agents to adjust their consumption profiles based on additional information about future shocks.
The necessary part of Theorem1describes scenarios in which the desire for intertemporal substitution fails to generate trade. Retrade can occur only if consumption smoothing motivates a change in allocations. If Condition 1 is violated,eA=eB, and there is no aggregate risk. Without aggregate risk at date 2, in any optimal allocation, each agent holds the same position in both stateAand stateBclaims. With identical positions across endowment states, the realization of endowment states (and signals) becomes irrelevant. The discussion of Theorem1suggests that the mechanism studied in this paper is not able to generate trade when there is no aggregate shock.
When condition 3 is violated, both agents have homogeneous preferences. They share the same attitudes about risk sharing and intertemporal substitution. Because the preferences characterized here are homogeneous of degree 1, agents share the same desire for intertemporal substitution (relative to the desire for risk-sharing) even under possibly different levels of wealth. After the arrival of information, both agents want to smooth their consumption in the same way such that there are no gains from trade.
In this section, I illustrate the possibility of information-based trading and the intertemporal substitution channel through a simple example. Consider the case in which the total endowment at date 1 ise1=1, and the date 2 total endowment is2516ifθ=Aand116ifθ=B. Agents 1 and 2 share the common prior thatπ0=12. At date 1, agent 1 observes a private signalx. Her posterior isπH=34ifx=H, andπL=14ifx=L.
Agents 1 and 2 share the same risk-aversion parameterα1=α2=12and the same discount rateβ1=β2=1, but have different attitudes about intertemporal substitution:ρ1=12andρ2=1.3At date 0, the initial allocation for agents is(e11,eA1,eB1)=(536,516,180)and(e12,eA2,eB2)=(3136,54,120), respectively. One can verify that the initial allocation is ex-ante Pareto-optimal. If the no-trade result holds, then the initial endowment is also the optimal allocation at date 1 andzi,1=0.
One can show, however, that given this initial allocation, agents have an incentive to trade after the arrival of information. If agent 1 observesx=H, then the agents' equilibrium consumption plans are(c11,cA1,cB1)=(61360,3051152,615760)and(c12,cA2,cB2)=(299360,14951152,2995760). If agent 1 observesx=L, then the optimal allocations become(c11,cA1,cB1)=(34360,85144,17720)and(c12,cA2,cB2)=(326360,3536,7180). To achieve these allocations, whenx=H,p1(H)=(1,1225,4), and agent 1 buys11360units of securityS1, sells551152units of securitySA, and sells115760units of securitySB. Whenx=L,p1(L)=(1,125,3), and agent 1 sells245units of securityS1, buys518units of securitySA, and buys190units of securitySB.
With the sameαi, agents trade because agent 1 is more concerned about consumption smoothing across dates. Becausep1(H)≠p1(L), market prices fully reveal the signalxas in standard REE models. Whenx=H, agent 1 believes that the future total endowment is likely to be high. To smooth her consumption over time, she wants to increase her current consumption. As a result, agent 1 has a strong incentive to sell some of her future endowment claims to increase her current consumption. Similarly, whenx=L, agent 1 infers that date 2's aggregate endowment is likely to be low. To smooth her consumption over time, she wants to increase her future consumption. She thus prefers to sacrifice her current consumption for more claims on the future endowment. In equilibrium, agent 2 is relatively less concerned about consumption smoothing, and she provides agent 1 with insurance for consumption smoothing through information-based trading.
Implementing this information-based trading plan at date 0 is impossible even if the financial market is complete. No security can pay one unit of date 1 consumption goods conditional on the realization of θ at date 2. After any possible trade at date 0, agent 1's position on date 1's consumption is a constant and thus is independent of signalx.
In this subsection, I derive some results on the relation between agents' preferences and trading. To simplify the analysis, I start with an ex-ante Pareto-optimal allocation.
I first characterize the relationship between date 1 trades.
Lemma 3.In equilibrium, if trade occurs, then



Proof.See AppendixA.□

The first part of Lemma3describes trading on date 2 consumption goods. For a risk-averse agent, an increase (decrease) in stateAconsumption makes stateBconsumption relatively more (less) attractive. Given the realization of signalx, if agentidecides to purchase (sell) securitySA, then she would also purchase (sell) securitySB. The second half of Lemma3suggests that if agentipurchases (sells) bothSAandSB, then she must sell (purchase) securityS1at the same time. This is straightforward given the binding budget constraints.
The next lemma characterizes the consumption allocation at posttrade date 2.
Lemma 4.In equilibrium, ifαi=αj, then



Proof.See AppendixA.□

Whenα1=α2, agents share the same degree of risk aversion. As a result, aggregate risk is shared equally between agents and each of them holds a constant fraction of future aggregate endowments across date 2 states. Ifαi>αj, agentiis relatively more risk-tolerant and takes more aggregate endowment risk by holding a relatively higher consumption ratiocAicBi. In this case, agents are trading equity shares.
Lemma4also suggests that information-based trade may involve rebalancing across endowment shocks. Since the key intuition behind such trade is based on new information generating a motive for intertemporal rebalancing, one may conjecture that information-based trade only involves reallocation among dates. This intuition, however, holds only when agents share the same attitude toward risk-sharingα1=α2. When agents have heterogeneous risk-aversion, the less risk-averse agent takes more risk on future endowment, and the realization of signalxaffects each agent's date 2 positions differently, introducing a need to rebalance across date 2 endowment states.
Proposition 1.In equilibrium, ifαi=αjandρi<ρj, then



Proof.See AppendixA.□

Proposition1characterizes trade between agents. Similar to the example studied in SectionII.B, when agents share the same risk-aversion parameter but have different attitudes about intertemporal substitution, aggregate shocks are shared equally. In equilibrium, each agent holds a constant share of future aggregate endowments across date 2 states. Agentiis relatively more concerned about intertemporal substitution. She would proportionally sell future endowment claims to increase her current consumption whenx=H.
The second part of Proposition1corresponds to cases in which agents have risk-aversion heterogeneity but share the same attitude about intertemporal substitution,ρi=ρj. Since agentiis less concerned about risk, she finds reallocation across dates less costly and thus is relatively more eager to smooth consumption. In equilibrium, she sells future endowment claims to increase her current consumption whenx=H. Theorem 2 also suggests that the mechanism predicts trading for consumption smoothing from agents who are more concerned about intertemporal substitution and/or less risk-averse. Agents less concerned about consumption smoothing and/or more risk-averse essentially provide a consumption-smoothing insurance through trading.
article.III. Discussions:
The financial market in my model is complete with respect to endowment states (as in Milgrom and Stokey (1982)) in the sense that securities have state-contingent payoffs at date 1 and at date 2 for both states. The key reason for trade is that all securities are measurable to the information filtration. As a result, no security generates different payoffs in current consumption goods contingent on future endowment states. However, agents want to correlate their current consumption plans with future endowment shocks. Trading based on signals connects consumption over time.
To illustrate, consider the following modification: reconfigure date 1 and date 2 consumption in the baseline model as two consumptions goods that agents consume in the final date 2. It is natural to conjecture that this static consumption modification should not affect the analysis because consumption in different periods can be interpreted as different consumption goods in the consumption bundle. However, it is straightforward to show that this two-good static case is just an example of Milgrom and Stokey (1982) and the no-trade theorem holds. The reason is that in the modified setting, both the information filtration and the set of available securities trading in the market change.
TableIshows the available securities in each case (when the market is complete for endowment states). In the dynamic consumption case, agents consume goodsc1at time 1, but θ is realized at time 2. The nature of time suggests that no security can make its current payoff conditional on states realized in the future, and there is only one security forc1. In the static consumption bundle case,S1,θis a security that pays one unit of consumption goodsc1(the one that is consumed in date 1 in the two-date case) when θ is realized. With two endowment states and two consumption goods, there are2×2=4different securities. Agents can achieve all desirable θ-dependent correlations between the two consumption goods through ex-ante trading.
When agents consume over time, any ex-ante allocation after date 0 trading must satisfy the condition thatc1(A)=c1(B). This payoff measurability restriction makes trades possible after the arrival of information at date 1. When agents learn the signal, they can trade conditional on the information; that is, they agree to make different trades according to the realizations ofx. Agenti's time 1 consumption then becomes a random variable related to signalx, which is statistically related to future endowment states θ. Thus, information-based trading may be mutually acceptable by expanding the set of possible consumption allocations, which may also change the Pareto frontier. In contrast to the existing literature (Blume, Coury, and Easley (2006)) that emphasizes mutual insurance opportunities, here the source of potential Pareto improvement is intertemporal substitution. Agents find information-based trades mutually acceptable because they enable agents to correlate their current consumption to future consumption shocks.
The baseline model specification is based on non-EU and is not state-additive, and hence one may wonder whether trades are driven by these features. In this section, I address this concern by presenting a simple example of information-based trades when agents have state-additive preferences that have an EU representation. This example demonstrates that trades are still possible with time-inseparable EU preferences. Because agents' preferences are state-additive and dynamically consistent, the mechanism studied here is different from the dynamic inconsistency channel in Dow, Madrigal, and Werlang (1990).
Consider a simple exchange economy in which the aggregate endowment at date 1 ise1=2and the total endowment at date 2 is 7 ifθ=Aand 4 ifθ=B. Agents 1 and 2 share the common priorπ0=12. At date 1, agent 1 observes a private signalx. For simplicity, I assume perfect signals, and thusπH=1andπL=0.
At date 0, the initial allocation among agents is(e11,eA1,eB1)=(1,194,238)and(e12,eA2,eB2)=(1,94,98). One can verify that the initial allocation is ex-ante Pareto-optimal andp0=(1,29,49). If the no-trade result holds, then the initial endowment is the optimal allocation at date 1 as well andzi,1=0.
However, one can show that given this date 0 allocation, agents have incentives to trade after the arrival of information. For instance, if agent 1 observesx=L, she believes that the future aggregate endowment is low, and thus, it is optimal for her to reduce her current consumption and hold more of securitySB. In equilibrium,p1(L)≈(1,0,0.9610)and agent 1's consumption plan changes to(c11,cA1,cB1)≈(0.9594,0,2.9100). To achieve this, agent 1 sells 0.0406 units of securityS1and buys 0.0350 units of securitySB. Similarly, one can characterize the trade whenx=H.
Given the existence of information-based trades, it is worth considering the relationship between the social value of information and trade. To define the social value of information, consider an economy identical to that in the baseline model except that no signal occurs at date 1 (equivalently, one can assume the signal to be uninformativeπH=πL=π0). I refer to this economy as the economy without signalx.
Definition 3.Information is socially valuable if both agents have higher utility at date 0 compared with the economy without signalx, with at least one agent having strictly higher utility.

This definition is similar to that used in Maurer and Tran (2016). Maurer and Tran (2016) study environments in which information is public and agents trade only once, at date 0 (date −1 in their setup). My model allows for possible date 1 retrading.
The following result shows that information must be socially valuable to generate trade.
Proposition 2.If trade occurs in equilibrium, the private signalxis socially valuable.


Proof.See AppendixA.□

In equilibrium, the price is fully revealing and trades are mutually acceptable. If retrading occurs, agents find it optimal to trade on signals to achieve a more efficient allocation. Information must be socially valuable, because the equilibrium allocation in the economy without signalxis feasible but is not the optimal choice for agents.
It is worth noting that information being socially valuable does not necessarily imply the existence of information-based trade. For example, if both agents have the same preferences and they both prefer early resolution of uncertainty, then from Theorem1, the no-trade result would hold regardless of the signal structure. However, if the signal perfectly reveals the future endowment shock, then the signal is socially valuable because both agents prefer early resolution of uncertainty.
article.IV. Conclusion:
In this paper, I study information-based trading in a multiperiod consumption setting. I show that in contrast to the celebrated no-trade theorem in single-period economies, information-based trades in a multiperiod consumption scenario can be mutually acceptable, because information about future consumption shocks allows agents to adjust their allocations to smooth consumption over time.
This paper suggests several promising directions for future research. For instance, studying strategic trading when agents care about consumption smoothing may provide new insights. Future research on the relationships between intertemporal substitution, trading volume, and dynamic trading patterns may also improve our understanding of markets.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong

Since the equilibrium is unique, at least one agent is strictly better off with trade.□
